MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION As To The First And Second Causes Of Action
Off Calendar Feb-18-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFFS CHARLES STINSON, AKA CHUCK STINSON AND MADELYN FOSSAT'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION is denied as to both the first and second causes of action. Plaintiffs failed to satisfy their initial burden of production as to either claim. A declaratory relief claim only lies where there is a controversy between the parties. Yet plaintiffs' moving papers are silent as to the position of Ms. Voisenat on the two issues for which it seeks declaratory relief and provides no facts to refute those positions. As to the first cause of action, nothing in plaintiffs' moving papers discuss the effect of the April 14, 2011 order on the enforceability of the lien, the asserted conduct of plaintiffs that Ms. Voisenat contends constitutes a waiver of plaintiffs' right to declare the lien unenforceable or the asserted conduct of plaintiffs that Ms. Voisenat contends constitutes a ratification of the lien. Without presenting any facts on those issues, plaintiffs cannot meet their summary adjudication burden that they are entitled to a declaration that the lien is invalid because of Ms. Voisenat's non-compliance with Rule 3-300. As to the second cause of action, plaintiffs' moving papers are silent as to the positon of Ms. Voisenat that she did not violate Business and Professions Code 6148(b) because she provided all required information while plaintiffs were her clients. In all events, based on the evidence presented by Ms. Voisenat, there are triable issues whether: 1) plaintiffs waived their right to invalidate the lien; 2) plaintiffs ratified the lien; 3) Mr. Knight's letter of April 9, 2012 constituted a request under 6148(b) and 4) even if it did, Ms. Voisenat violated 6148(b) vis-a-vis that letter. Moreover, the court construes section 6148(b) as imposing obligations only as to current clients, not former clients. ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication On The Second Amended Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Aug-05-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Geoff Wiggs, of Law Offices of Geoff Wiggs, Tel. 650.577.5952, for plaintiffs;
Marc Voisenat, Attorney at Law, Tel. 510.272.9710, for defendants.

Ruling: A R G U E D. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: DEFENDANT PHYLLIS VOISENAT'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication On The Second Amended Complaint is denied. Defendant Voisenat failed to meet her burden of production or comply with CRC 3.1350(b). The res judicata and merger/bar doctrines do not apply to this action. Plaintiffs Stinson and Bonjean and Defendant Voisenat were not adverse parties in the underlying litigation and Judge Mason did not adjudicate whether the lien was enforceable. The court has subject matter jurisdiction over this action. The ethical rules and Business & Professions Code sections are merely cited in the declaratory relief causes of action to help determine whether the parties' attorney/client agreement is enforceable and whether Defendant Voisenat has a viable lien. The declaratory relief causes of action are well pleaded and Plaintiffs allege an actual controversy between the parties within the meaning of CCP 1060. The court rejects Defendant's argument that the real party in interest is not prosecuting the action and/or an indispensable party is missing. Plaintiffs Stinson/Bonjean did not need to name Julius Castle Restaurant, Inc. in this action. It is a defunct entity, Stinson/Bonjean were the sole owners of the entity, and Stinson/Bonjean were parties to the fee agreement. There is no chance that Voisenat will be subject to multiple conflicting claims. Defendant Voisenat argues that the statute of limitations bars the claims, but her moving papers do not clearly articulate when the claims accrued and the actual controversy between the parties is ongoing. Defendant Voisenat fails to show that the accounting claim fails as a matter of law because the parties shared a fiduciary relationship and the amount of credit, if any, that Plaintiffs are entitled to is unclear. Defendant's argument that the litigation privilege (CC 47) bars this action has no merit. Plaintiffs are seeking an adjudication of their rights under the attorney/client fee agreement. Order signed in open court. Judge Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk Felicia Green; Court Reporter Melanie Gheno, CSR No. 7489; Dept. 302. (302/EHG)

RULING: There being no objection, the court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT PHYLLIS VOISENAT'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication, as follow: The court treats the motion for summary judgment or alternatively, summary adjudication as a motion for judgment on the pleadings. See White v. County of Orange (1985) 166 Cal.App.3d 566, 569 (disagreed with on other grounds in John R. v. Oakland Unified School Dist. (1989) 48 Cal.3d 438). Defendant's motion largely addresses pleading defects that can be cured. The court grants the motion for judgment on the pleadings with 20 days leave to amend. The court vacates the existing trial date. Defendant can respond to the second amended complaint as she sees fit, including renewing her motion for summary judgment. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT.
